subject: math
title: PSLE Math — Geometry: Angles & Shape Properties

stats.headline: Roughly **7-8 marks per Paper 2** come from heavy angle questions — plus another 5-7 marks from the bread-and-butter 1-2 mark angle basics. Geometry is one of the highest-yield PSLE Math topics. The trick: PSLE marks not just the answer but the REASON — citing the rule by name is half the mark in most cases.

keyConcepts.title: Welcome — Geometry: Angles & Shape Properties
keyConcepts.body: Geometry questions look harmless at first glance — find the missing angle. But PSLE markers grade the WORKING, not just the number. A student who writes "55°" with no reason caps at 1 mark. The same student who writes "55° (angles on a straight line)" scores full marks.
bullets: **The 5 patterns this class drills:**
• **Pattern A** — Angle basics: straight line, vertically opposite, angles at a point. 180° vs 360° trap.
• **Pattern B** — Parallel lines: alternate (equal) vs co-interior (sum to 180°). Naming the right rule = the mark.
• **Pattern C** — Quadrilateral hides an isosceles triangle (the most common shape PSLE tests, 12 of 19 heavy Qs).
• **Pattern D** — Two-shape glue: equilateral + rhombus / square + parallelogram. Angle-chase across the shared vertex.
• **Pattern E** — Folded paper: reflection creates equal sides AND equal angles.
**The one habit that adds 3-5 marks**: write the rule next to every step.

Example: "∠ABC = 180° − 110° = 70° (angles on a straight line)"
NOT just: "∠ABC = 70°"
keyConcepts.callout: Every 4-5 mark geometry question stacks 3 or more angle facts. Naming each rule as you go is what separates 2/5 (right answer, no reasons) from 5/5 (right answer, all reasons cited).

keyConcepts.title: Pattern A — Angle basics (the bread-and-butter 1-2 markers)
keyConcepts.body: 15 one-mark + 28 two-mark angle basics across PSLE 2016-2025 — about 70 marks total. These are "free marks" IF your child can tell the 4 rules apart at a glance.
bullets: **Rule 1 — Angles on a straight line = 180°**. Look for two lines meeting where one continues straight through.
**Rule 2 — Vertically opposite angles are EQUAL**. Look for an X-cross of two lines. The angles across from each other are equal.
**Rule 3 — Angles at a point = 360°**. Look for 3+ lines all meeting at the same vertex.
**Rule 4 — Right angles = 90°**. The small square in the diagram is the giveaway.

**The 180° vs 360° trap (PSLE 2020 Q23):** when the angle asked is the BIG one (the reflex angle around the outside of a triangle/shape), the rule is 360°, not 180°. Students automatically subtract from 180° and lose 2 marks.

scoringExample.scenario: ABC is an equilateral triangle. A line is drawn from A at an angle of 65° to side AB. The angle ∠x is the BIG angle measured around point A, outside the triangle. Find ∠x.

fullMarks.label: 2 marks
fullMarks.text: ∠x = 360° − 60° − 65° = 235° (angles at a point) Because x is the BIG angle going around point A, the rule is 360° not 180°.

keyConcepts.callout: First question to ask: "Is this angle inside the shape or outside? Is it across an X-cross? On a straight line?" Pick the right rule BEFORE you do any arithmetic.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern B — Parallel lines: name the rule
keyConcepts.body: You already know the 3 parallel-line rules. The PSLE-specific trap: students get the right number but skip naming the rule in the working — and lose the method mark.
bullets: **Alternate (Z) = equal**  ·  **Co-interior (C) = 180°**  ·  **Corresponding (F) = equal**
**The marking-point keyword is the rule name in brackets.** E.g. "= 72° (co-interior angles, DE // CF)". Without the bracket, the marker awards the answer mark only — never the method mark.

scoringExample.scenario: ABCD is a parallelogram. AED and BFC are straight lines. ∠DEF = 108° and ∠BFA = 90°. Find ∠AFE. (Real PSLE 2017 Q27, 2 marks.)

fullMarks.label: 2 marks
fullMarks.text: ∠CFE = 180° − 108° = 72° (co-interior angles, DE // CF) ∠AFE = 180° − 90° − 72° = 18° (angles on a straight line)

keyConcepts.title: Pattern C — Quadrilateral hides an Isosceles (most-tested pattern)
keyConcepts.body: The most common heavy geometry pattern (12 of 19 four-mark+ questions in 2016-2025). Equal-side marks INSIDE a named quadrilateral → spot the isosceles triangle → solve via base angles + triangle sum 180°.
bullets: **The trap:** trapeziums in PSLE are USUALLY NOT isosceles. Only claim base angles equal when the equal-side mark is stated.
**The other trap:** "AB = AC = CD" doesn't make ABC equilateral — only the named pairs are equal.

scoringExample.scenario: ABCD is a trapezium with AB parallel to DC. AFB and EFC are straight lines, AE = EB, and ∠EBC = 94°. Find ∠AEF. (Real PSLE 2021 QP2-13, 4 marks.)

fullMarks.label: 4 marks
fullMarks.text: ∠EAB = 180° − 94° = 86° (co-interior, AE // BC, since trapezium has AB // DC and EF is the transversal) ∠EBA = ∠EAB = 86° (isosceles triangle, AE = EB → base angles equal) ∠AEB = 180° − 86° − 86° = 8° (triangle sum 180°) ∠AEF = 180° − 8° = 172° (angles on a straight line, AFB)

keyConcepts.title: Pattern D — Two shapes glued together (rhombus + parallelogram)
keyConcepts.body: Look out for the hidden isosceles triangle. Then chain the rhombus + parallelogram interior-angle rules — opposite angles equal, adjacent angles sum to 180°.

scoringExample.scenario: ABCD is a parallelogram and DEFG is a rhombus. CDE is a straight line. ∠ABC = 51°, ∠GAD = 25°, ∠FGE = 35°. Find ∠AGF. (Real PSLE 2016 QP2-7, 4 marks — part (b).)

fullMarks.label: 4 marks (the actual answer key)
fullMarks.text: ∠GEF = ∠FGE = 35° (base angles of an isosceles triangle — rhombus side EF = side GF) ∠GFE = 180° − 35° − 35° = 110° (sum of angles in a triangle) ∠GDE = ∠GFE = 110° (opposite angles of a rhombus) ∠CDA = ∠CBA = 51° (opposite angles of a parallelogram) ∠ADG = 180° − 51° − 110° = 19° (angles on a straight line CDE) ∠AGD = 180° − 25° − 19° = 136° (sum of angles in triangle ADG) ∠DGE = (180° − 110°) ÷ 2 = 35° (base angles of isosceles triangle DEG) ∠AGF = 360° − 136° − 35° − 35° = 154° (angles at a point G)

keyConcepts.title: Pattern E — Folded paper (same trick, but equilateral)
keyConcepts.body: Folding preserves side lengths — the folded side equals the original side. Look for a hidden EQUILATERAL triangle this time (3 sides equal → all 60°), then use the original shape's 90° corners to finish.

scoringExample.scenario: Square PQRS. The line GH divides the paper into two equal parts. The paper is folded so corners P and Q meet at point E on GH. Which of these are true? A. ∠MRE = 30°.   B. ∠RMG = 105°.   C. RSE is an equilateral triangle. (Real PSLE 2025 Q14, 2 marks.)

oneMark.label: 1 mark (spotted equilateral but no follow-through)
oneMark.text: C is true — RSE is equilateral.

fullMarks.label: 2 marks (the actual answer key)
fullMarks.text: ER = ES = RS  (folding preserves side length — P went to E, so ER = PR = RS; similarly ES = QS = RS) → RSE is an equilateral triangle. So statement C is true. ∠ERH = 60° (angle of an equilateral triangle, since ∠ERS = 60° within the square's right angle ∠SRH) ∠MRE = (90° − 60°) ÷ 2 = 15°  (NOT 30°, so A is false) In triangle MRH: 180° − 90° − 15° = 75° ∠RMG = 180° − 75° = 105°  (angles on a straight line) → B is true Answer: B and C only.

keyConcepts.title: Let's get some practice!
keyConcepts.body: Time to put it together. This quiz pulls real PSLE-style geometry questions across all 5 patterns plus the 5-mark killer structure.
bullets: **10 MCQ + 5 OEQ** — a mix across angle basics, parallel lines, quadrilateral-isosceles, two-shape glue, and folded paper.
After you submit, the AI looks at where you went wrong and suggests a follow-up focused quiz on just those patterns.
Mistakes auto-saved — we'll come back to them in 1 week.

cta.label: Click here to start your practice quiz

keyWords.definition: Two angles sharing a straight line as their boundary sum to 180°. Most common geometry rule — appears in almost every PSLE Paper 2 angle question.
Angles meeting at a single vertex sum to 360°. Used when the angle asked is the BIG (reflex) angle going around the outside of a shape.
When two lines cross to form an X, the angles across from each other are EQUAL. Look for the X-cross in the figure.
Z-shape between two parallel lines. The two angles on OPPOSITE sides of the transversal are EQUAL. Cite 'alternate angles, [parallel pair]' to earn the method mark.
C-shape between two parallel lines. The two angles on the SAME side of the transversal SUM TO 180°. Cite 'co-interior angles, [parallel pair]'.
A triangle with TWO sides equal. The two BASE angles (across from the equal sides) are EQUAL. PSLE marks this property explicitly — name the equal sides as the justification.
A triangle with all THREE sides equal. All 3 angles are 60°. Use as a 'free anchor' whenever it appears in a figure.
A 4-sided shape with all 4 sides equal. Opposite angles are equal. Adjacent angles sum to 180°. Diagonals bisect at 90°.

subTopics.label: Pattern A — Angle basics (straight line, vertically opp, 360°)
subTopics.description: Tests 1-2 mark basics: angles on a straight line (180°), vertically opposite (equal), angles at a point (360°), right angles (90°). The 180° vs 360° trap is the most-missed.

subTopics.label: Pattern B — Parallel lines (alternate vs co-interior)
subTopics.description: When the question says 'AB parallel to CD', test the student's ability to pick the correct rule. Alternate (Z) = equal. Co-interior (C) = 180°. Corresponding (F) = equal. Rule-naming is the method mark.

subTopics.label: Pattern C — Quadrilateral hides an Isosceles triangle
subTopics.description: The most-tested heavy pattern. Named quadrilateral + equal-side mark inside → isosceles triangle. 3-step chain: quad property → spot isosceles → triangle sum 180°. 4-mark structure.

subTopics.label: Pattern D — Two shapes glued together (angle-chase)
subTopics.description: Two named figures share a side (equilateral + rhombus, square + trapezium, etc.). Anchor on the equilateral 60° or square 90°, walk angles through the shared vertex using straight-line / 360° / rhombus properties.

subTopics.label: Pattern E — Folded paper geometry
subTopics.description: Trending up. A shape is folded along a line so a corner meets another point. Folded sides = original sides (length preserved). Angles mirror across the fold. Use to identify equilateral/isosceles in the new shape.
